turns.prompt: You won on a promise to cut taxes. Voters expect relief. Schools, hospitals, and roads expect funding.

choices.label: Cut public services to fund tax reductions
Cut taxes and borrow to cover the gap
choices.flavour: Have the cake. Eat it. Invoice later.

delayed.headline: Borrowing to fund tax cuts starts to spook markets

choices.reaction: Consumers spend. Bond traders reach for their spreadsheets.

choices.label: Shift the burden with a wealth tax instead of income cuts
choices.reaction: Middle earners cheer. Asset holders threaten exit.

turns.prompt: Tax cuts appear in payslips. Approval rises — until the first service failures hit the news.

choices.label: Privatise underperforming services
choices.reaction: Queues shorten for those who can pay. Others fall behind.

choices.label: Launch a public-sector efficiency drive
choices.flavour: Do more with less — on paper.
choices.reaction: Consultants arrive with slide decks. Frontline staff update their out-of-office.

choices.label: Retarget tax relief to low earners only
choices.reaction: Poorest households gain. High earners feel misled.

turns.prompt: Credit agencies warn: revenue falls short of projections. Something must give before the next budget.

choices.label: Partially reverse tax cuts to stabilise finances
choices.flavour: Break a promise to keep the books.

choices.label: Sell state assets to plug the hole
choices.reaction: One-off cash arrives. Strategic capacity leaves.

choices.label: Double down — cut taxes further to spur growth
choices.reaction: Shopping tills ring. Economists argue on television — nobody changes channel.

turns.prompt: A school roof collapses. A hospital cancels surgeries. The trade-off is no longer abstract.

memory_callbacks.text: The service cuts you made to fund tax relief are now headline tragedies.
Borrowing you used to defer pain is now due with interest.
The wealth tax you introduced drove capital offshore before revenue landed.

choices.label: Emergency levy to restore critical services
choices.flavour: Take back with one hand what you gave with the other.
choices.reaction: Services reopen. The mandate feels damaged.

choices.label: Accept service collapse in poorer regions
choices.reaction: Wealthy districts cope. Others empty out.

turns.prompt: Final reckoning. What size of state did you leave?

choices.label: Entrench a small-state, low-tax model
choices.reaction: Entrepreneurs pop champagne. The library closes on Tuesdays — permanently.

choices.label: Rebuild robust public services even if taxes rise
choices.reaction: Institutions recover. Paychecks shrink — and so does patience.

choices.label: Settle on a mixed public-private model
choices.flavour: Neither pure market nor pure state.
choices.reaction: Your postcode now predicts your life chances. Politicians call it pragmatism.
